intro: Sizing your AC correctly from the start saves money and hassle—avoiding multiple trips to the hardware store and excess units. This calculator determines your room's cooling needs using industry-standard ratios: 600–800 BTU per square meter, adjusted for sunlight exposure and insulation quality. Free and fast. Values are updated for 2026 and reviewed regularly.
keyTakeaway: Formula: **Room Air Conditioning BTU Calculation**. Direct application with your input values. Cooling requirement: 600–800 BTU/m² based on sunlight exposure and insulation level.
useCases: Technical calculations for room air conditioning sizing
Teaching and learning AC sizing principles
explanation:
**Room air conditioning sizing** relies on established industry ratios and standards (BTU per square meter, insulation factors, sunlight adjustments). These calculations follow IRAM standards and best practices used by HVAC professionals.

## How It's Calculated

Room AC cooling requirements are calculated by multiplying your room area by the cooling demand factor (BTU/m²). Standard guidance: 600–800 BTU per square meter, adjusted for sun exposure and insulation quality.

This calculator applies the formula with your inputs and checks against typical ranges, alerting you to any unusually high or low values.

## Final Notes

This calculation is a reference guide only. For critical decisions (major purchases, complex installations, or energy goals), consult an HVAC professional. Values are current as of 2026 and reviewed regularly.

faq.q: How many BTU do I need for my room?
faq.a: It depends on room size and conditions. Standard guidance is 600–800 BTU per square meter. Rooms with high sun exposure or poor insulation need more; well-insulated rooms need less.

faq.q: What size air conditioner should I buy?
faq.a: Calculate your room's square meters, then multiply by the cooling load factor (600–800 BTU/m² depending on sun and insulation). An HVAC technician can help you choose the right unit.

faq.q: How are frigorías different from BTU?
faq.a: Frigorías (frig) is the metric unit; 1 frigoria = 1 kcal/h. BTU/h is the imperial equivalent. The conversion is approximately 1 frigoria ≈ 3.97 BTU.

faq.q: Why do sun exposure and insulation affect AC requirements?
faq.a: Rooms with more direct sunlight gain more heat and require higher cooling capacity. Poor insulation allows heat to enter more easily. Both factors increase your AC's cooling demand.
Results are practical estimates (±5–10% variation depending on specific materials and conditions). For precise sizing, consult an HVAC professional.

faq.q: Is the calculator free and do you store my data?
faq.a: Yes, completely free with no registration required. All calculations happen in your browser—we never send data to any server.

faq.q: What's the difference between BTU per m² and total BTU?
faq.a: BTU per m² is the cooling density (capacity per unit area). Total BTU is your room's complete cooling requirement, calculated by multiplying room area × cooling density.

faq.q: Should I oversize my air conditioner?
faq.a: Slight oversizing (10–15%) is acceptable and provides a safety margin. Major oversizing wastes energy and creates uneven cooling. Follow professional sizing guidelines.

sources.name: NIST — National Institute of Standards and Technology
